w:r.w:t: Full-Stack | TypeScript + Python | 14 Pages | No Docker Constraint

The Registration page is the entry point for all new users — students, drivers, and admins. It collects identity information, validates it client and server side, creates a database record, and issues a JWT pair so the user lands directly on their role-appropriate dashboard without a separate login step.
All frontend logic — type-safe form state, validation, API calls
Fast dev server + production bundle (zero-config TS support)
Component tree: RegistrationForm, FieldInput, RoleSelector, SubmitButton
Uncontrolled form state, field-level validation, submit handling
Client-side schema — mirrors server Pydantic schema exactly
Form layout, input states (error/success/focus), responsive design
Field error shake, success checkmark, page transition fade
Store JWT tokens + user object after successful registration
TypeScript runtime for auth + API layer
Request body validation — rejects malformed payloads before DB touch
Insert users + students/drivers rows in PostgreSQL transaction
Hash password with cost factor 12 before storage
Sign RS256 access token (15min) + refresh token (7 days)
Send verification email — optional for production v1
Max 5 registration attempts per IP per hour
Submit registration form — create user, issue JWT pair
Real-time email availability check on field blur
id, email, hashed_password, role, created_at, verified=false
Single transaction with student/driver row below
Only if role=driver — bus assigned later by admin
Fills name, email, password, role, college_id fields
React Hook Form + Zod client validation
Queries users table by email, returns { available }
Inserts users + students/drivers in DB transaction
Returns 201 with token pair + user object
Stores tokens in Zustand, sets httpOnly cookie via API
Password stored as bcrypt hash with cost factor 12 — never plain text, never MD5/SHA1.
Email check endpoint is rate-limited (20 req/min per IP) to prevent email enumeration.
college_id and phone number are hashed (SHA-256) before storage — not searchable in plain form.
JWT uses RS256 (asymmetric) — private key signs, public key verifies — private key never leaves server.
Registration endpoint rate-limited to 5 attempts/hour/IP to prevent mass account creation.
For production v1, email verification is optional but recommended. The verified flag in the users table is pre-built for this — flip it to required when email relay is configured.

The Login page authenticates returning users, issues a short-lived access token and a long-lived refresh token, and routes them to their role-appropriate dashboard. The token strategy is designed for production security: access tokens live only in JS memory (never localStorage), refresh tokens live in httpOnly cookies (XSS-proof).
Typed login form state, token storage, role routing
Controlled email + password fields with error states
Client-side: email format + password non-empty before POST
Store access_token + user object in memory
Dark login card, input focus ring, error state styling
Card fade-in, wrong-password shake, loading spinner
Compare submitted password vs stored hash — timing-safe
Sign + verify RS256 access token (15min) + refresh token (7 days)
Set HttpOnly Secure SameSite=Strict cookie for refresh token
Store access token JTI → user_id for instant revocation
Find user by email, fetch role + profile
10 login attempts per IP per 15 minutes
Submit credentials — returns access + refresh token
Exchange refresh token for new access token (silent renewal)
Revoke refresh token — clears Redis session + DB record
Restore session on page load — returns user object
// refresh_token set as httpOnly cookie — NOT in response body
Process death — user re-auths on tab close (acceptable)

Never store tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage. Both are accessible to any JS on the page — one XSS vulnerability exposes all users. JS memory (Zustand) is wiped on tab close, which is intentional for a campus app.
Fetch hashed_password + role for credential check
Store new refresh JTI, check revocation on /auth/refresh
Access token JTI → user_id (900s = 15min). DEL on logout.
Refresh token JTI → user_id (7 days). DEL on logout.
Live map, stop ETA, SOS shield
Check-in button, route view, late night status
Fleet overview, ghost bus alerts, toggle panel

The Live Map View is the primary student interface. It shows a real-time animated bus marker on a campus map, driven by a persistent WebSocket connection. The marker never freezes — client-side dead reckoning projects its position every 100ms regardless of network quality. A confidence circle grows around the marker as signal weakens, and a status bar communicates system state honestly.
All map logic, WS handling, dead reckoning loop
MapPage, BusMarker, ConfidenceCircle, StatusBar, ETACard components
Declarative Leaflet map — MapContainer, TileLayer, Circle, Marker
Free tile layer — no API key required
100ms position projection from last known speed + heading
Poll GET /api/bus/{id}/position every 30s if WS drops
Dark map overlay, frosted-glass ETA card, status bar
ETA card slide-up, status bar slide, marker drop bounce
Bearer token sent in WS upgrade header
WS /ws/bus/{bus_id} — authenticate, subscribe Redis channel, relay
Subscribe to channel:bus:{id}, read state/position hashes
Verify Bearer token in WS Upgrade header before connection
Forward /api/eta/* requests to Python ML microservice
RandomForestRegressor loaded at startup from eta_model.joblib
Load trained model file — inference in <5ms
Read bus state/position/meta for ML feature assembly
// Inbound — GPS Simulator → Fastify WS handler
// Outbound — Fastify → all connected frontend clients
const { lat, lng, heading, speed } = useVehicleStore.getState();
const dist = (speed / 3.6) * elapsed; // speed in km/h → m/s
// On real WS ping — divergence check
All real-time map updates — state, position, tier, confidence_radius_m
Set starting lat/lng before WS stream begins
Polling fallback — lat, lng, heading, ts
Trigger ghost bus alert overlay if non-empty
Render stop markers + route polyline on map
ETA card — eta_min, eta_max, leave_now, confidence
Vehicle state — drives marker color and status bar
Pub/sub broadcast — Fastify relays to all WS clients
Stop coordinates + names for map overlay
requestAnimationFrame runs at display refresh rate (60fps) but dead reckoning only updates position every 100ms — throttled with a timestamp check to avoid over-computation.
Leaflet marker setLatLng() is called only when position delta exceeds 0.5m — prevents redundant DOM updates during very slow movement.
Redis pub/sub broadcast is fire-and-forget — Fastify WS handler does not await acknowledgement from frontend, preventing head-of-line blocking across connected clients.
Stop + route data is fetched once on load and cached in Zustand — never re-fetched during the session unless the user switches routes.

The Stop-Centric View is IZUM's product reframe — instead of showing where the bus is, it answers the only question students actually care about: should I leave now? Each stop card shows an ETA range, a Leave Now / Wait recommendation, and a signal confidence indicator. No map, no complexity — pure decision support.
Typed stop cards, ETA polling, leave-now logic
StopList, StopCard, ETABadge, LeaveNowPill, ConfidenceRing, BottomNav
GET /api/eta/{stop_id} — polled every 30s per visible stop
Card layout, Leave Now amber pulse, confidence ring colors
Staggered card slide-up on load, card expand on select, badge pulse
Client-side fuzzy search over stop names
Forward ETA request to Python ML microservice
Cache ETA responses per stop per bus for 15s (reduces ML calls)
Verify Bearer token on every ETA request
Fetch all stops with route assignment
// Client-side — runs on every ETA update
const calcLeaveNow = (eta_min: number, walkTime: number): LeaveStatus => {
// walkTime comes from user preference stored in Zustand
// User can adjust via settings slider
Render all stop cards with names + routes
stop_id, name, lat, lng, route_id, sequence — all stop cards
Route name shown on each card
Cached ETA response — prevents hammering ML service every poll

The ETA Detail Page gives the full picture for a single stop — the ML model's confidence range, all 8 input features exposed for transparency, a visual uncertainty bar that widens as signal degrades, and an honest fallback message when the model is operating on dead reckoning. It is both a student-facing tool and a judge-facing proof of the ML layer's depth.
Typed ETA detail response, uncertainty bar width calc, state transitions
Gradient background, confidence bar track + fill, signal dot states
ETA number blur-morph, uncertainty bar width transition, banner slide-down
This page's primary endpoint is served by the Python FastAPI ML microservice, not the Node.js Fastify server. The Fastify server proxies the request and forwards the response.
GET /eta/{stop_id}/detail — assemble features, call model, build response
Project position from last known speed + heading when gap > 90s
Read bus:state, bus:position, bus:meta for feature vector
Query avg arrival times by stop + hour + day for fallback ETA
base_width = 2.0 # minutes — minimum range at perfect signal
All ETA data: range, features, dead_reckoning, uncertainty_bar_pct
Stop name + route name for page header
signal_tier, ping_gap_seconds — primary ETA model inputs
current_speed, lat, lng — velocity + location features
Stop name, route name — header display
AVG arrival time by stop_id + hour + day_of_week — fallback ETA

The Status Bar is a zero-backend overlay component that rides on the existing WebSocket stream. It surfaces system state honestly — network degradation, buffer count, ghost bus flags — through carefully designed text and animation that never alarms unnecessarily. No dedicated API calls. No extra DB reads. Pure frontend state machine.
Typed state enum, message map, animation triggers
StatusBar component — conditionally rendered above ETA card
vehicleState, bufferCount, ghostBusActive — read from WS message handler
AnimatePresence for mount/unmount, layout animation for height
Frosted glass, colored left border accent per state, text slide
Reads from same WS connection opened by Page 1 — no new connection
Read .state + .buffer_count on every message — no dedicated call
Ghost bus flag — if anomalies.length > 0, activate GHOST_BUS bar

This component has zero dedicated backend surface. It is entirely driven by the WS message stream already open on the map page. The ghost bus poll is the only additional HTTP call — and it is shared with Page 6 (Ghost Bus Alert).

The Network Toggle Panel is the demo control centre — the tool judges use to simulate network degradation live. It is protected behind admin/demo role and shows real-time stats as the mode changes. Tapping a card fires a POST to the Python simulator service which reconfigures its RTT target, payload tier, and ping interval immediately.
Typed mode enum, stats polling, counter animation
Card ripple on tap, glow pulse on active, number count-up on stats change
Dark panel, colored card borders, animated gradient background on active card
Route guard — redirect to /map if role !== admin|demo
POST /api/simulator/network-mode — validate + forward to Python simulator
Verify Bearer token, check role claim = "admin" or "demo"
Receive mode change, adjust RTT target, payload size, ping interval
Read bus:{id}:meta every 2s for live stats — no compute needed
{ mode, interval_s, payload_bytes } — update active card highlight
{ rtt_ms, tier, buffer_count } for animated counter display
rtt_ms, tier, buffer_count — read every 2s for live stats counters
ZSET size — buffer queue count shown in "Buffer Queue" counter
Current state — shown as WebSocket status indicator dot

The Ghost Bus Alert detects buses that have been stationary for 15+ minutes with zero speed variation — a sign the bus is not running. A background task on the Node.js server scans Redis state periodically, flags anomalies, and the frontend polls for them. When detected, a modal rises from the bottom with a 60-second auto-dismiss and a "Report to Admin" button that writes to PostgreSQL.
Typed anomaly interface, 60s countdown timer, auto-dismiss logic
anomalies[], dismissedIds[] (persist dismissed so modal doesn't reappear)
Modal bounce-in from bottom, ghost float loop, countdown SVG ring drain
Dark backdrop, frosted modal card, SVG countdown ring
Every 60s: scan all bus:{id}:state keys in Redis for ghost conditions
Read bus position + speed history to detect zero-speed pattern
INSERT anomaly_log + student reports to PostgreSQL
Bearer token required for POST /report — get student_id from JWT
[ { bus_id, type, stationary_minutes, flagged_at } ] — triggers modal
last_ping_ts, route name — shown in modal body text
speed field — primary ghost detection input

The Silent SOS is designed for one scenario: a student on a bus who feels unsafe but cannot visibly call for help. Three rapid taps on a near-invisible shield icon triggers a 10-second cancel window, then silently POSTs GPS location and bus ID to campus security. No red SOS text. No alarm sound. No visible response to a bystander.
Tap counting logic, 10s cancel timer, vibration, POST with location
Silent haptic feedback on trigger — mobile only
Get current lat/lng at moment of trigger — fallback to bus position
POST /api/sos after 10s window, only if not cancelled
Screen flash (100ms), cancel bar drain, toast slide-up, cancel retract
Shield opacity-20 (nearly invisible), cancel bar thin red strip at top
Extract student_id from JWT sub claim server-side — never from body
INSERT sos_events row — student_id hash, bus_id, lat, lng, ts, status
Send security alert email within 2s of SOS receipt
After 10s cancel window, if not cancelled
{ sos_id, status: "dispatched" } — log internally, show "Help on way" toast
At moment of trigger — get real-time pos
lat, lng embedded in SOS payload (fallback to geolocation API)
sos_id, student_id_hash, bus_id, lat, lng, ts, status, cancelled
Fallback lat/lng if geolocation API is unavailable or denied

The Virtual Escort page lets a parent or warden track a student's bus journey live from a browser — no app install, no login. The student generates a token-based URL that expires in 2 hours. The escort page polls position every 15 seconds and displays a read-only map with a blue bus marker, route progress bar, and a "Journey Complete" state on arrival.
Typed escort token response, share sheet integration
Native share sheet on mobile — share URL to WhatsApp/SMS directly
Bearer token required to generate link
Token extraction from URL, 15s polling loop, journey complete detection
Read-only map — blue bus marker, route polyline, stop markers
Banner slide-down, progress dot glow, journey complete fade transition
Token in URL path is the only auth — no login required
Extract student_id from Bearer token for generate endpoint
INSERT escort_tokens, SELECT + validate on position endpoint
Read bus:{id}:position for lat/lng relay to escort viewer
{ lat, lng, current_stop, eta_min, eta_max, journey_complete }
Stop list for route progress bar render
token, student_id, bus_id, destination_stop_id, expires_at, used
Real-time lat/lng relayed to escort viewer on each 15s poll
If LOST/BUFFERING, escort viewer shows "Signal weak" notice
Stop names + sequence for route progress bar

Late Night Mode activates automatically after 21:00. It overlays a deeper dark theme with blue-purple tint on the main map, adds a driver check-in countdown ring that depletes every 10 minutes, and monitors route deviation. A missed check-in fires a security alert immediately. Route deviation beyond 100m triggers a map highlight showing expected vs actual path.
Time-based activation, countdown ring state, deviation highlight logic
Add expected route polyline (blue) + actual path (red) on deviation
Moon banner slide-down, ring depletion (SVG strokeDashoffset), overdue shake + glow, deviation badge animate-in
Deep dark overlay (bg-slate-950), blue-purple tint, red ring on overdue
Every 60s: check if driver check-in was received within window
POST /checkin requires valid driver JWT — reject student tokens
INSERT driver_checkins, SELECT route waypoints for deviation calc
Email campus security on missed check-in or deviation > 100m
Read/write latenight:{bus_id} hash — last_checkin_ts, overdue flag
Full audit trail — driver_id, bus_id, ts, late_night_mode=true
Route waypoints — used to calculate expected bus position at any timestamp
last_checkin_ts, overdue(bool), deviation_detected — fast UI reads
Actual lat/lng — compared against expected route for deviation calc
Ring turns amber, text "Check-in due soon"
Ring stops, pulses rapidly, security alert fired, shake animation

The ThinkRoot landing page is IZUM's public face — a product launch site, not a README. It must convince judges in 30 seconds that IZUM solves a real problem with real technical depth. Every section is scroll-triggered, the hero animates on load, and the feature cards communicate the three core differentiators visually.
Hero animation sequencer, scroll observer, typewriter, map preview logic
Static site output — single HTML file, optimised CSS/JS bundles
whileInView triggers — slide-in story, flip-in cards, fade-in stats
Character-by-character tagline reveal — pure CSS animation fallback
Miniature looping demo map in hero — animated marker, state changes
Subtle animated particle field in hero — <10KB, tree-shaken
Dark theme (#0a0a0f), neon green accents, frosted glass cards
Static deploy from Vite dist/ — no server required
Letter fade-in + typewriter + map preview loop
One paragraph: student missing bus in rain on college WiFi
3 cards flip up staggered on scroll
94% payload reduction / Dead reckoning / Honest ETA
Count-up animation on scroll into view
Pulse glow animation, color shift on hover
Architecture / API Reference / Tech Stack links
Check live demo reachability — show/hide "Live Demo" button based on result
Vite tree-shaking, tsParticles lite variant, dynamic imports for Leaflet
Tailwind purge — only used utility classes included
No hero image — pure CSS/JS animation, no file to load
tsParticles lite — not the full 200KB build
React.lazy() — Leaflet loads only after hero text is visible

The Architecture page is where judges go to verify technical depth. It shows the full system flow with animated data-flow dots, the vehicle state machine with colour-coded states, and a section-by-section explanation of every technology choice. Everything is scroll-triggered and hover-interactive — it feels like a real engineering blog post, not a hackathon submission.
SVG animation control, hover tooltip logic, scroll observer
System flow diagram — nodes, connectors, animated data-flow dots
Dots moving along SVG paths — pure CSS, no JS animation library needed
Positioned tooltips on SVG node hover — explains each component
whileInView — each section reveals on scroll
State transition arcs — force-directed layout for 5 state nodes
Dark bg, monospace labels, green accent connectors
GPS Sim → Fastify → Redis → ML → Leaflet
Hover node → tooltip with tech choice rationale
Click state → highlight all transitions, show trigger condition
Bar animates to final width on scroll into view
Hover service → show port + health check
Why Fastify / Why Redis / Why Prisma
Expand on click — show code example inline
Page is fully static — no API calls
All diagrams rendered client-side from hardcoded data

The Payload & API Docs page is the technical reference — payload comparison, network tier table, ML feature set, and a full endpoint accordion. It is designed for judges who want to verify the implementation details claimed in the brief. Every code block has a copy button. Every table row highlights on scroll. The accordion gives judges instant access to any endpoint's request/response shape.
Accordion state, row highlight timing, copy-to-clipboard
Copy button on all code blocks — async write with visual feedback
Table rows fade + slide in one-by-one on scroll into view
AnimatePresence height animation — each endpoint expands/collapses
Server-side syntax highlighting at build time — zero runtime JS
Dark code blocks, green endpoint badges, monospace font
Connection protocol, inbound/outbound message schemas, 5 vehicle states
Zod schema, bcrypt cost, JWT pair response, 409 duplicate handling
Credential check flow, httpOnly cookie strategy, role routing
8-feature ML vector, confidence_width formula, 4 display modes
Full model_features object, dead_reckoning flag, uncertainty_bar_pct
4 modes, RTT targets, payload sizes, interval changes
Privacy design, student_id hashing, 10s cancel, dispatch flow
Token validation, position relay, journey_complete detection
Ghost bus detection logic, stationary_minutes threshold
Late night mode trigger, overdue logic, security alert
Fully static page — all content hardcoded from documentation
No live API calls — works even when backend is offline
React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind, Framer Motion, Zustand, Axios, React Router, React Leaflet, React Hook Form, Zod, react-hot-toast
Fuse.js (Page 2), tsParticles (Page 10), Floating UI (Page 11), D3 (Pages 9+11), Shiki (Page 12)
Fastify, @fastify/websocket, fastify-rate-limit, fastify-cookie, Prisma, ioredis, jose, bcrypt, Resend, Axios
FastAPI, Uvicorn, scikit-learn, joblib, redis-py, Pydantic v2
Fleet registration, campus route waypoints, stop coordinates
Safety event log, shareable escort links with expiry
Late night audit trail, ghost bus + deviation event log
JWT refresh token store for rotation and revocation
Real-time vehicle state — expires via TTL to drive state transitions
ZSET GPS event buffer during DEAD mode — priority flush on reconnect
JWT session tracking — DEL on logout for instant revocation
Pub/sub broadcast channel — Fastify fans out WS messages
Late night mode state — fast read for UI without PG query
15s ETA response cache — prevents ML service saturation
IZUM | Detailed Page Documentation Vol. 3 of 3 | Production | ThinkRoot × Vortex Hackathon 2026
